steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage ends: ‘Since molecular analysis does not require distinguishing subtle physiological differences, it is ______’. The blank must follow logically from that premise. If molecular analysis skips the need to spot fine physical differences, then it can handle cases where morphological methods would struggle — namely, species that look nearly identical.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘more likely to properly distinguish between morphologically similar but distinct invertebrate species than is the method used to identify Prionospio branchilucida’: Prionospio branchilucida was identified solely by morphology because molecular sampling was impracticable. Since morphology requires distinguishing subtle physical differences and molecular analysis does not, molecular analysis would be better at telling apart look-alike species. This follows directly from the passage’s logic. ✓
  • Choice B — ‘more frequently used for identifying species with physiological characteristics like those of Ledella knudseni’: The passage says nothing about usage frequency. It only discusses what molecular analysis requires, not how often it is used. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘better suited to distinguishing Prionospio branchilucida from morphologically similar species than to distinguishing Ledella knudseni’: This reverses the logic. Molecular analysis was impracticable for Prionospio branchilucida, so claiming it is especially suited for that species contradicts the passage. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘capable of providing greater physiological detail about a species’: The passage says molecular analysis does not require distinguishing physiological differences — that means it sidesteps physiology, not that it reveals more of it. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
